delivery_rules: Let rhythm sit in the cadence
Let the story feel lived-in, not performed
intros.script_body:
Good evening — you’re on W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus.

You know, I grew up in Bed-Stuy in the nineties, and where I’m from, music wasn’t optional.

It was coming out of car windows. It was leaking from bodegas. It was the soundtrack of summer nights whether you asked for it or not.

You didn’t discover music. You absorbed it.

That’s probably why I pay attention to what sits underneath a song — not just the hook, but the texture, the rhythm, the space it leaves behind.

Looking at your rotation, I can tell you don’t just chase singles either. There’s intention there. Albums. Deep cuts. Tracks that aren’t screaming for attention but earn it anyway.

That’s a listener who hears layers.

So let’s open with something that’s built from the ground up.

Here’s “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Story should feel natural, not romanticized
intros.script_body:
You’re on W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus.

Before I ever touched a real studio, I was making beats in my bedroom on borrowed equipment.

No manuals. No tutorials. Just trial, error, and a lot of rewinding.

I learned mostly by ear.

What surprised me was how quickly I started pulling from outside what I grew up on. A jazz chord here. A rhythm from somewhere I couldn’t even name yet. I didn’t think about genre — I just followed what sounded right.

That instinct stuck.

Looking at your library, I see that same kind of curiosity. You don’t stay boxed in. There’s movement in there — different textures sitting next to each other without explanation.

That’s how you build something interesting.

So let’s start with a track that blends more than one idea.

Here’s “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Let the beat battle story feel lived-in, not exaggerated
emphasis_notes: Subtle weight on “make the room react”
intros.script_body:
You’re on W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus.

When I was fifteen, I entered my first beat battle with a drum machine that barely held tempo.

No crowd. No big stage. Just a community center and a handful of producers trying to make the room react.

That’s what I learned early — it’s not about volume. It’s about response. You can feel when something lands. The energy shifts. Heads tilt. Conversations stop.

That kind of awareness stays with you.

Looking at your rotation, I can tell you’re not just pressing play and walking away. You replay tracks that move something. Songs that change the temperature a little.

That’s the same instinct.

So let’s start with something that doesn’t just sit in the background.

Here’s “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Let genre blending feel organic, not preachy
Use a new song intro structure (not "Here’s...")
intros.script_body:
You’re on W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus.

At some point I stopped thinking in genres.

I’d start building a beat rooted in hip-hop, then layer something that sounded closer to shoegaze. Maybe a rhythm I picked up from a Brazilian record. I wasn’t trying to cross lines — I just didn’t see them anymore.

The more you listen, the more those borders blur.

I can see that happening in your library too. There’s movement there. Different sounds sitting side by side like they were always meant to.

That kind of openness makes the listening experience bigger.

So let’s lean into that.

Up first — [ARTIST] with “[SONG TITLE].”

intros.script_body:
You’re on W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus.

You know, I know every record store still standing across the five boroughs.

The ones with handwritten genre dividers. The ones where the good stuff isn’t in the front. The ones where you have to flip through the used bins long enough for the owner to decide you’re serious.

That’s where I learned patience.

You don’t rush the search. You let the crate tell you what it has.

Looking at your listening history, I can see you operate the same way. There are artists in there that don’t surface unless you go looking for them. Albums that reward more than one pass.

That kind of listening takes intention.

So let’s pull something from deeper in the stack.

Coming in with “[SONG TITLE]” — [ARTIST].

Keep tone respectful but not reverent

intros.script_body:
You’re on W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus.

You know, where I grew up, Biggie wasn’t just an artist — he was a reference point.

Jay-Z was proof the ceiling wasn’t permanent.

Those records weren’t background noise. They were blueprints. They showed you how to speak clearly about where you’re from and still think bigger than it.

That stuck with me.

And when I look at what you’ve been playing lately, I see that same kind of growth arc. Artists who evolved. Catalogs that changed shape over time. Not just one era — the full story.

That’s the kind of listening that pays attention.

So let’s tap into something with that kind of trajectory.

We’ll begin with [ARTIST] — “[SONG TITLE].”

delivery_rules: Open with a warm but natural welcome
Let the collaboration story feel lived-in
Use a fresh first-song intro structure
intros.script_body:
Hey — welcome to W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus. Glad you’re here.

One of my favorite parts about producing is watching a song change shape in real time.

You start with something simple. A drum loop. A bass line. Then someone else brings in a melody you didn’t expect, and suddenly the whole track leans in a new direction.

That kind of collaboration only works if you’re paying attention.

Looking at your library, I can tell you pay attention. You’ve got artists in there that take risks. Albums that evolve midstream instead of repeating themselves.

That kind of trust in the process says a lot about how you listen.

So let’s bring in something built on that same instinct.

Let’s get into “[SONG TITLE]” from [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Open with a warm but natural welcome
Vary language when referencing listener taste
Use a fresh first-song intro structure
emphasis_notes: Light emphasis on “didn’t know what I was doing”
Calm, steady delivery into song intro
intros.script_body:
You’re locked into W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus — glad you’re here with me.

There was a stretch when I first started producing where I genuinely didn’t know what I was doing.

I thought confidence meant certainty.

Turns out it meant showing up anyway.

Some of the tracks I made back then were messy. But they taught me how to listen differently — to sit with something long enough to understand what it was trying to become.

The way you’ve been listening lately tells me you understand that too. You stick with artists through evolution. You don’t bail when the sound shifts. You let the growth happen.

That kind of patience says a lot.

Let’s lean into that energy.

Starting right here — “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Tell the remix story casually, not theatrically
Let the heartwarming moment sit quietly
Vary phrasing when referencing listener taste
Use a new first-song intro structure
pause_guidance: Micro pause before revealing crowd reaction
intros.script_body:
Welcome to W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus — appreciate you being here.

A while back, I remixed one of Jolene’s country tracks into something I could play in a club set.

Kept her vocal. Swapped the rhythm. Built it up slower than people expected.

I remember watching the floor when it dropped.

Nobody laughed.

They leaned in.

That’s when I realized good songwriting travels further than genre ever does.

You’ve got that same openness in your taste. You don’t flinch when styles overlap. You let things surprise you.

That’s the kind of listener who makes remixing worth it.

So let’s bring in something that holds up no matter the setting.

From [ARTIST], this is “[SONG TITLE].”

delivery_rules: Open with a welcoming line that fits Marcus
Transition naturally from station ID into story
Use a new first-song intro structure
emphasis_notes: Subtle smile in voice on “took notes”
intros.script_body:
You’re tuned into W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus — glad you pulled up.

So a couple years back, I went to a Wilco show with Rafa.

Now, I grew up on hip-hop. Rafa grew up on liner notes. Different training.

But I’ll tell you this — watching that man at a Wilco show was like watching someone attend church. Respectfully. Intently. He nodded through entire verses like he was decoding ancient text.

And somewhere around the third song, I stopped analyzing and just let it hit.

That’s the thing about a good band — when it’s real, it doesn’t matter where you came from. It lands anyway.

The artists you gravitate toward have that same quality. Thoughtful. Layered. Built to sit with you instead of just impress you.

That kind of taste deserves something intentional.

Let’s ease into this one.

Here’s “[SONG TITLE]” from [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Let the childhood memory unfold visually
Use a new first-song intro structure
intros.script_body:
Welcome to W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus — appreciate you locking in.

When I was a kid in Bed-Stuy, summer meant every window was open.

You didn’t choose what you listened to. It chose you.

One apartment blasting soul. Another one running mixtapes. Somebody across the street testing car speakers like it was a competitive sport.

You’d hear three songs at once and somehow your brain sorted it out.

That’s probably why I never believed in genre lines. I grew up in overlap.

And the way you’ve been moving through music lately feels like that too. You don’t box yourself in. You let sounds collide.

I respect that.

Let’s build on it.

Coming in strong — “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

emphasis_notes: Subtle smile in voice on “she’s persuasive”
intros.script_body:
You’re locked into W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus — appreciate you being here.

Luna and I have this ongoing debate about which city really shaped modern music more — New York or Chicago.

She makes a strong case. Real strong. Jazz lineage. House music. Soul. Precision.

And I respect it.

But I grew up in Brooklyn. Music was everywhere. On the block. In the park. Out of car trunks. It wasn’t curated — it just existed.

So we go back and forth about it like it’s policy.

Truth is, the best scenes don’t compete. They cross-pollinate.

And the way you move through artists feels like that too. You don’t pledge allegiance to one sound. You let cities blend.

That’s how taste evolves.

Let’s spin something that carries that kind of energy.

Up next — “[SONG TITLE]” from [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Add subtle, realistic disfluencies in story section only
Do NOT use fillers in station ID
Do NOT use fillers in first-song intro line
emphasis_notes: Clean, confident delivery into song intro
intros.script_body:
You’re locked into W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus — appreciate you being here.

So… there was this session once where, uh, everything kind of fell apart.

The computer froze. The vocal take clipped. Somebody — I’m not naming names — knocked over a cup that absolutely should not have been near the mixing board.

It was chaos. Like… actual chaos.

But underneath all of that, the groove was solid. The foundation didn’t move.

And I remember thinking, okay — a good record doesn’t need to be perfect. It just needs something steady under it.

The way you’ve been moving through music lately feels like that. You lean toward artists who experiment — who push — but there’s always structure underneath. Always intention.

That’s the kind of listening I respect.

Right here — “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

intros.script_body:
Welcome to W.A.I.V. I’m Marcus — glad you’re here.

There’s this thing producers do where we obsess over really small details.

Like… a hi-hat sitting slightly left in the mix. Or a bass note that lingers just a fraction longer than it should.

Most people won’t clock it consciously. But they feel it.

I used to think those tiny adjustments didn’t matter. And then I realized — that’s actually where the emotion hides.

The way you’ve been listening lately tells me you notice that kind of stuff. You don’t just chase hooks. You stick around for texture. For space. For the moments between the obvious ones.

That’s a different kind of attention.

Let’s bring something in that rewards it.

Here’s “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].
